item.title: Langflow CVE-2026-5027 Exploitation, Miasma Leak, and PeopleSoft ShinyHunters Attacks
item.description: This digest window was dominated by active exploitation, developer ecosystem compromise, and policy movement around faster remediation of high-risk vulnerabilities. Reporting highlighted attacks against Langflow and Oracle PeopleSoft, continued evolution of self-propagating supply-chain malware,...
item.content:encoded:
This digest window was dominated by active exploitation, developer ecosystem compromise, and policy movement around faster remediation of high-risk vulnerabilities. Reporting highlighted attacks against Langflow and Oracle PeopleSoft, continued evolution of self-propagating supply-chain malware, and defensive changes planned for npm in response to recent package ecosystem abuse. Attackers are actively exploiting CVE-2026-5027, a high-severity path traversal flaw in Langflow that allows arbitrary file writes on exposed servers. The issue affects the POST /api/v2/files endpoint, where the filename parameter in multipart form data is not properly sanitized. Tenable disclosed the vulnerability on March 27, 2026, after reporting it to Langflow more than two months earlier without receiving a response; Snyk reported fixes in langflow-base 0.8.3 and Langflow 1.9.0, with 1.10.0 listed as the latest release. Separately, ShinyHunters claimed responsibility for ongoing data theft attacks against Oracle PeopleSoft cloud and on-premises customer instances. The group claimed to have stolen data from 300 PeopleSoft instances across more than 100 organizations, with education reportedly making up most of the affected sector. The actor described its exploitation path as a “gadget chain” involving old and zero-day vulnerabilities, with impact dependent on instance configuration. Researcher Michael R identified exposed online directories containing apparent attack tooling related to the PeopleSoft activity, including MeshCentral agents, a defacement and credential spray script, and staging materials. The two cases underscore continued attacker focus on externally reachable enterprise platforms where file write, deserialization, or configuration-dependent exploit chains can quickly become data theft or persistence opportunities. The Miasma credential-stealing framework was briefly exposed through GitHub repositories named “Miasma-Open-Source-Release” across numerous compromised developer accounts. SafeDep assessed that Miasma appears to be an evolution of the earlier Shai-Hulud worm, sharing many features, techniques, and code. The framework steals build environment and cloud credentials, compromises legitimate repositories and packages, and publishes trojanized versions to reach downstream developers. A notable feature of Miasma is its use of GitHub itself for command-and-control-like functions, reducing dependence on separate infrastructure. The leaked code also revealed a dead-man switch capable of deleting files in a user’s home and Documents folders if a stolen GitHub token used for exfiltration is revoked. This behavior combines credential theft, repository abuse, and destructive pressure against victims attempting to cut off attacker access. GitHub announced npm v12 security changes intended to disrupt similar supply-chain tactics. In npm v12, npm install will not run dependency preinstall, install, or postinstall scripts unless explicitly approved, and it will no longer fetch dependencies from Git repositories or remote URLs such as HTTPS tarballs unless permitted. GitHub said the defaults could have disrupted techniques seen in malicious install scripts, data-stealing npm packages, and Git dependency abuse documented in Shai-Hulud attacks. JFrog’s discussion of IDC’s 2026 Software Engineering Challenges Report framed Shadow AI as a growing unmanaged enterprise risk, replacing Shadow IT as developers use personal accounts, unvetted plugins, and AI-generated code in production workflows. The report, based on a survey of 1,000 enterprise and mid-sized organizations, described a widening gap between AI adoption goals and governance readiness. The article argued that AI agents cannot be assumed to self-police compliance because their behavior is non-deterministic and may bypass policies that exist only as documentation or informal agreements. Aikido described a parallel shift in where code is written, citing PostHog’s observation that roughly as many pull requests are being merged through Slack as through the code editor. The article said developers increasingly write code through AI agents, chat interfaces, and MCP servers that may be outside security team visibility. It connected this dispersion to recent supply-chain incidents such as TeamPCP activity, the Axios maintainer account hijacking, and the Glassworm attack, which emphasized compromise of developer devices and accounts rather than application vulnerabilities. Developer endpoints were characterized as high-value control points because they often hold cloud credentials, SSH keys, npm publish tokens, Kubernetes configs, source code access, and production access. The reporting also noted limitations in traditional MDM and EDR visibility for malicious packages, IDE extensions, browser plugins, and postinstall hooks before credential theft occurs. CISA issued a new binding operational directive requiring U.S. federal civilian agencies to patch certain high-risk vulnerabilities within 72 hours. The accelerated timeline applies when vulnerabilities meet three of four criteria: public internet exposure, presence in the Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog, automated exploitability, and the degree of adversary control over the vulnerable system. Agencies have 180 days to adopt the new time frame. The directive also requires agencies to assess when and how a vulnerable system may have been compromised before patching, reflecting CISA’s position that applying a patch does not generally evict an existing threat actor. CISA also urged state, tribal, and local governments, along with critical infrastructure owners and operators, to adopt similar practices.

Path traversal flaw in AI dev platform Langflow exploited in attacks — Must Read

Attackers are actively exploiting CVE-2026-5027, a Langflow path traversal flaw that can let unauthenticated attackers write arbitrary files on exposed servers. Langflow users should upgrade to the latest release immediately.

  • Attackers are actively exploiting CVE-2026-5027, a high-severity path traversal vulnerability in Langflow, to write arbitrary files on exposed servers.
  • The flaw affects Langflow's file upload functionality because the POST /api/v2/files endpoint does not properly sanitize the filename parameter in multipart form data.
  • Tenable publicly disclosed CVE-2026-5027 on March 27, 2026, after reporting it to the Langflow team more than two months earlier without receiving a response.
  • Snyk Security reported that the issue was fixed in langflow-base version 0.8.3 and in the Langflow application version 1.9.0.
  • Langflow users are recommended to upgrade to the latest release, version 1.10.0.

Oracle PeopleSoft servers hacked in ShinyHunters data theft attacks — Must Read

ShinyHunters claims it is stealing data from Oracle PeopleSoft cloud and on-premises servers using a mix of old and possible zero-day flaws, with more than 100 organizations allegedly affected. PeopleSoft operators should check logs for the reported IP indicators and begin incident response if they see related activity.

  • The ShinyHunters extortion gang told BleepingComputer it is behind ongoing data theft attacks targeting Oracle PeopleSoft cloud and on-premises customer instances.
  • ShinyHunters claimed it stole data from 300 PeopleSoft instances across more than 100 organizations, with most impacted organizations in the education sector.
  • The threat actor said it is using a "gadget chain" of old and zero-day vulnerabilities, and that exploitation may depend on how a PeopleSoft instance is configured.
  • Cybersecurity researcher Michael R found exposed online directories containing attack-related tooling, including MeshCentral agents, a defacement and credential spray script, and staging materials.
  • Organizations running Oracle PeopleSoft are advised to analyze logs for connections from the listed IP addresses and begin incident response if the indicators are found.

The ‘Miasma’ worm source code briefly leaked on GitHub — Should Read

The Miasma credential-stealing worm framework briefly leaked on GitHub, exposing code that can steal developer/cloud credentials, hijack repositories and packages, and self-propagate through open-source supply chains. The leak may fuel more advanced copycat attacks against npm, PyPI, RubyGems, GitHub, and CI/CD environments.

  • The Miasma credential-stealing attack framework was briefly open-sourced on GitHub through repositories named "Miasma-Open-Source-Release" on numerous compromised developer accounts.
  • Miasma appears to be an evolution of the earlier Shai-Hulud worm and shares many of the same features, techniques, and code.
  • The malware steals build environment and cloud credentials, compromises legitimate repositories and packages, and publishes trojanized versions to infect downstream developers.
  • SafeDep found that Miasma requires no command-and-control infrastructure because it uses GitHub for that purpose.
  • The leaked source code revealed a dead-man switch that can delete files in a user's home and Documents folders if a stolen GitHub token used for exfiltration is revoked.

GitHub announces npm security changes to tackle supply-chain attacks — Should Read

GitHub’s upcoming npm v12 will block dependency install scripts, Git-based dependencies, and remote URL dependencies from running or resolving automatically unless explicitly approved. The change is meant to reduce npm supply-chain attacks that abuse npm install for automatic code execution.

  • GitHub announced that npm v12, expected next month, will introduce security-focused changes aimed at blocking supply-chain attacks that abuse behaviors triggered by the npm install command.
  • Starting in npm v12, npm install will not run preinstall, install, or postinstall scripts from dependencies unless they have been explicitly approved, including native module builds through node-gyp and prepare scripts from Git, local file, and linked dependencies.
  • npm install will no longer fetch dependencies from Git repositories or resolve dependencies installed from remote URLs, including HTTPS tarballs, unless they are explicitly permitted.
  • GitHub says the new defaults could have disrupted recent supply-chain attack techniques involving malicious install scripts, data-stealing npm packages, and Git dependency abuse documented in Shai-Hulud attacks.
  • GitHub recommends developers upgrade to npm 11.16.0 or newer to see warnings about actions that will break under npm v12 and identify workflows that will require explicit approval.

CISA to require federal agencies to patch some cyber vulnerabilities within 3 days — Should Read

CISA will require federal civilian agencies to patch the highest-risk, internet-facing and actively exploited vulnerabilities within 72 hours, with agencies given 180 days to implement the new process. The directive is meant to prioritize fixes for vulnerabilities that can be automated and rapidly exploited, especially as AI increases attacker capabilities.

  • CISA issued a new binding operational directive requiring federal civilian agencies to patch certain high-risk cyber vulnerabilities within 72 hours.
  • The 72-hour deadline applies to vulnerabilities that meet three of four criteria: public internet exposure, inclusion in the Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog, automated exploitability, and the level of adversary control over the vulnerable system.
  • Federal agencies have 180 days to adopt the new patching time frame under the directive.
  • The directive also requires agencies to check when and how a vulnerable system was compromised before applying a patch, because applying a patch generally does not evict a threat actor.
  • CISA is strongly urging state, tribal, and local governments, as well as critical infrastructure owners and operators, to adopt similar vulnerability management practices.

Code is being written everywhere, and the device is the only constant — Optional

As developers increasingly write and approve code through Slack, AI agents, MCP servers, and other non-IDE interfaces, the developer device remains the critical attack surface where credentials and supply-chain risks converge. The article argues security controls must monitor installs and activity at the device level because traditional MDM and EDR often miss malicious packages, extensions, and agent-driven workflows until after compromise.

  • PostHog co-founder James Hawkins said the company is merging roughly as many pull requests through Slack as through its code editor, reflecting a shift toward dispersed coding interfaces.
  • The article says developers are increasingly writing code through AI agents, chat interfaces, and MCP servers that many security teams have not seen or approved.
  • The article cites TeamPCP activity, the Axios maintainer account hijacking, and the Glassworm attack as examples of supply-chain incidents that targeted developer devices rather than vulnerabilities in application code.
  • Developer devices are described as holding cloud credentials, SSH keys, npm publish tokens, Kubernetes configs, source code access, and production access, making their compromise high-impact.
  • The article argues that MDM and EDR may not see or block malicious packages, IDE extensions, browser plugins, or postinstall hooks before credentials are stolen.

The Governance Gap: What IDC’s 2026 Data Reveals About AI and the Software Supply Chain — Optional

IDC’s 2026 data suggests enterprise AI adoption is moving faster than governance, leaving software supply chains exposed to unmanaged “Shadow AI” and risky AI-generated code. The key takeaway is that organizations need platform-level guardrails in delivery pipelines rather than relying on developers or AI agents to self-police compliance.

  • JFrog hosted a virtual panel discussion with IDC’s George Mironescu and JFrog executives Yuval Fernbach and Asaf Barkan about enterprise AI adoption, pipeline governance, and software delivery.
  • The IDC 2026 Software Engineering Challenges Report surveyed 1,000 enterprise and mid-sized organizations globally and identifies a widening gap between corporate AI ambition and governance readiness.
  • The article states that Shadow AI has replaced Shadow IT as the primary unmanaged threat inside enterprises, with developers using personal accounts, unvetted third-party plugins, and AI-generated code in production environments.
  • The article argues that AI agents cannot be trusted to self-police compliance because they operate non-deterministically and may bypass policies that exist only in documentation or verbal agreements.
  • The recommended path forward is to build platform-level guardrails at the software supply chain layer to manage AI adoption within corporate security and governance practices.

item.title: JDY Botnet, Langflow CVE-2026-5027 Exploitation, and Critical Ivanti/Fortinet/SAP Patches
item.description: This coverage window was dominated by AI-adjacent security risk, active exploitation of exposed software, and renewed attention on edge and third-party access. Reporting also highlighted a China-linked reconnaissance botnet expanding across SOHO and IoT infrastructure, alongside a disruptive...
item.content:encoded: This coverage window was dominated by AI-adjacent security risk, active exploitation of exposed software, and renewed attention on edge and third-party access. Reporting also highlighted a China-linked reconnaissance botnet expanding across SOHO and IoT infrastructure, alongside a disruptive cyberattack affecting Australian sugar production. Multiple articles focused on how AI agents challenge existing zero trust and security inspection models. Varonis and Netskope both described AI agents as non-human actors that authenticate, call APIs, access databases, invoke tools, and exchange data at machine speed, creating gaps in policies designed around human access and authentication-time controls. Reported threat scenarios included prompt injection, tool poisoning, identity and privilege abuse, memory poisoning, supply chain compromise, and uninspected Model Context Protocol or app-to-LLM traffic. Software supply chain exposure in AI-assisted development also featured prominently. Socket announced an integration with Replit intended to evaluate open source packages as they enter AI-powered build workflows, citing threats such as typosquatting, impersonated packages, malicious transitive dependencies, credential stealers, and install scripts that retrieve second-stage payloads. The reporting reflects a broader shift in which AI agents can recommend, install, and wire dependencies directly into applications. SpecterOps published research on SQL Server 2025 AI-related features, demonstrating offensive use cases involving spinvokeexternalrestendpoint, CREATE EXTERNAL MODEL, and AIGENERATEEMBEDDINGS. The proof-of-concept activity included HTTPS exfiltration of table contents and files, trigger-based persistence for newly inserted data, NTLM coercion over SMB via UNC paths for ONNX models, and command-and-control patterns disguised as embedding-style traffic. Langflow CVE-2026-5027 stood out because it is reported as unpatched and under active exploitation. Tenable described the flaw as a path traversal issue in the POST /api/v2/files endpoint caused by insufficient sanitization of multipart filename values, while VulnCheck reported that default unauthenticated auto-login can allow attackers to obtain a session token and reach remote code execution. Censys data cited in the reporting identified roughly 7,000 internet-exposed Langflow instances, with most in North America. Patch reporting covered several critical enterprise products. Fortinet fixed CVE-2026-25089, a CVSS 9.1 command injection flaw in FortiSandbox products; Ivanti fixed CVE-2026-10520, a CVSS 10.0 unauthenticated root-level command injection issue in Ivanti Sentry, and CVE-2026-10523, a CVSS 9.9 authentication bypass enabling creation of arbitrary administrative accounts. SAP also released fixes for critical vulnerabilities affecting NetWeaver AS ABAP and ABAP Platform, SAP Commerce Cloud, SAP Data Hub, and NetWeaver Application Server Java, with no exploitation reported for those listed issues. CISA added Cisco, Chrome, and Arista vulnerabilities to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog. The additions included CVE-2026-20245 in Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN Manager, CVE-2026-11645 in Google Chrome V8, and CVE-2026-7473 in Arista EOS. Arista stated it does not plan to patch CVE-2026-7473 due to potential configuration breakage and instead documented ACL-based mitigations. Black Lotus Labs reporting, covered by both The Hacker News and BleepingComputer, described the China-linked JDY botnet as having expanded to more than 1,500 compromised SOHO and IoT devices. The botnet is used as a centrally controlled reconnaissance network for service discovery, banner grabbing, TLS certificate collection, protocol fingerprinting, and flaw-focused scanning after public vulnerability disclosures. The botnet has expanded beyond earlier Cisco RV320 and RV325 router activity to include devices from Araknis, Mimosa Networks, Ubiquiti, DrayTek, Hikvision, Linksys, and Cisco. Researchers said JDY places strong emphasis on U.S. targets, including U.S. military and associated networks. Command infrastructure reportedly uses Tor hidden services, and some activity involves the open-source Platypus reverse-shell and host-management framework. A cyberattack disrupted Mackay Sugar operations in Queensland, forcing shutdowns at the Farleigh and Racecourse mills and prompting growers to stop harvesting sugarcane until further communication. The company said it engaged cybersecurity experts and local authorities, implemented temporary measures to keep essential operations running, and was working to restore affected systems. Mackay Sugar did not disclose whether ransomware, data theft, or another form of malicious activity was involved. Identity and access management remained a parallel theme, particularly around third parties and account verification. Health-ISAC released a white paper on third-party IAM risks in healthcare, emphasizing vendor, partner, remote access, connected device, and facility management complexity. Separate reporting on identity verification cited a 160% surge in credential theft in 2025 and described phishing-resistant MFA, service desk verification, device trust signals, and passkeys as major areas of focus in modern identity programs.

item.title: CVE-2026-42897, CVE-2026-45657, and AI-Enabled Phishing Drive June Threat Coverage
item.description: This digest window was dominated by Microsoft’s unusually large June security release, including an actively exploited Exchange Server zero-day and a critical Windows kernel issue described as wormable. Reporting also highlighted continued evolution in phishing and initial access tradecraft, with...
item.content:encoded: This digest window was dominated by Microsoft’s unusually large June security release, including an actively exploited Exchange Server zero-day and a critical Windows kernel issue described as wormable. Reporting also highlighted continued evolution in phishing and initial access tradecraft, with AI-generated sites, adversary-in-the-middle kits, event-themed fraud, and low-content email payloads complicating detection. Microsoft patched CVE-2026-42897, an actively exploited high-severity spoofing vulnerability in Exchange Server affecting Exchange Server 2016, Exchange Server 2019, and Exchange Server Subscription Edition. The flaw can be exploited remotely without privileges by sending a specially crafted email that, under certain Outlook Web Access interaction conditions, executes arbitrary JavaScript in the user’s browser context. CISA added the vulnerability to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog on May 15 and set a May 29 remediation deadline for U.S. federal agencies. Microsoft’s June Patch Tuesday fixed more than 200 security flaws, described by multiple vulnerability trackers as the largest monthly release in the program’s history. Among the most severe was CVE-2026-45657, a critical Windows kernel vulnerability rated 9.8 that could allow remote full system compromise without user interaction and was characterized by ZDI as wormable. The release also included CVE-2026-41091, an actively exploited Microsoft Defender elevation-of-privilege flaw that could allow an attacker with an existing foothold to gain full control of a system. The same Microsoft coverage included three disclosed zero-days, including CVE-2026-50507, a BitLocker bypass tied to the pseudonymous researcher Nightmare Eclipse. Separately, Microsoft reported that a small percentage of devices upgraded to Windows 11 versions 24H2 or 25H2 may fail to install the latest cumulative update, with errors including 0x80073712 and 0x800f0993 and logs referencing component store corruption or missing hydration candidates. Zscaler ThreatLabz reported that phishing activity declined by roughly 20% year over year in both 2024 and 2025, while attackers shifted toward more targeted and personalized lures resembling routine business workflows. The report identified 413,524 AI-generated site instances, with 37,447, or 9.06%, flagged as malicious, and attributed builders including Manus AI, Blackbox AI, and Anything AI. ThreatLabz also found that 95.2% of phishing activity was delivered over encrypted channels. The same research described phishing kits using adversary-in-the-middle and browser-in-the-middle techniques to capture credentials and MFA codes during live login flows, enabling session-level compromise rather than simple password theft. ThreatLabz also recorded 89.9 million hostile interactions with external decoys over six months and observed more than 121,000 distinct AWS-hosted IPs probing customer environments, underscoring the scale of opportunistic reconnaissance against exposed infrastructure. Event-themed fraud is already appearing around the FIFA World Cup 2026. The FBI warned that threat actors are creating fraudulent FIFA-affiliated websites to collect personal data, conduct financial fraud, and sell fake goods or services, while Cyble confirmed that many FBI-flagged domains remained active at publication time. Reported examples included ww-fifa[.]com, a fake hospitality and ticket portal requesting personal and payment details, and fifaworldcup-careers[.]com, a fake recruitment domain associated with multiple IP addresses and wildcard SSL certificates. IRONSCALES analyzed an “ACH payment” email sent from carlaburbo@keystonenatural[.]com to an accounts receivable team, with ap@keystonenatural[.]com copied. The message passed SPF, DKIM, DMARC, and composite authentication at final delivery, while original authentication results showed dkim=none and dmarc=none, and ARC validation at i=2 returned cv=fail, indicating a broken forwarding chain of trust. The email’s payload consisted entirely of a single PNG attachment named image002.png, with no message body, no links, and no OCR-extracted text. Themis flagged the message at 54% confidence as credential theft, and one mailbox was quarantined, illustrating how image-only payloads and authentication-chain inconsistencies can coexist with apparently successful final-delivery authentication outcomes. A related control-validation theme appeared in reporting on automated pentesting limitations. The article argued that clean automated pentest results can create false confidence because they show exploitable paths but not whether SIEM rules fired, EDR alerts were raised, or SOC analysts had sufficient signal to act. It contrasted automated pentesting with breach and attack simulation, framing the latter as focused on whether controls blocked, detected, logged, or missed known malicious behaviors.

item.title: Microsoft YellowKey, phpBB RCE, ServiceNow Exploitation, and Ivanti Sentry Root RCE
item.description: This coverage window is dominated by high-impact vulnerability disclosures affecting widely deployed enterprise and community platforms, including Windows, phpBB, ServiceNow, and Ivanti Sentry. Several items involve either confirmed exploitation, public proof-of-concept activity, or flaws that...
item.content:encoded: This coverage window is dominated by high-impact vulnerability disclosures affecting widely deployed enterprise and community platforms, including Windows, phpBB, ServiceNow, and Ivanti Sentry. Several items involve either confirmed exploitation, public proof-of-concept activity, or flaws that enable full system or administrative compromise. A parallel theme is the increasing role of advanced AI systems in vulnerability discovery and the controls vendors are placing around cyber-capable models. Microsoft’s June 2026 Patch Tuesday addressed three zero-day vulnerabilities known as GreenPlasma, MiniPlasma, and YellowKey. GreenPlasma and MiniPlasma are local privilege escalation flaws in the Collaborative Translation Framework and Cloud Files Mini Filter Driver, respectively, and can allow attackers to obtain SYSTEM privileges on fully patched Windows systems prior to remediation. YellowKey is a Windows Recovery Environment backdoor affecting unpatched Windows 11 and Windows Server 2022/2025 systems, enabling attackers with physical access to bypass BitLocker protections. The vulnerabilities were disclosed by a researcher using the handle Nightmare Eclipse, who framed the release as a protest against Microsoft Security Response Center disclosure practices. The same researcher has reportedly released proof-of-concept exploits for additional Windows zero-days, including BlueHammer, RedSun, UnDefend, and RoguePlanet. This creates a broader exposure pattern in which defenders face not only patched vulnerabilities, but also an expanding set of public exploit artifacts tied to unresolved or recently disclosed Windows flaws. Aikido reported a critical phpBB authentication bypass vulnerability that can lead to remote code execution and complete takeover of the underlying system. The flaw affects phpBB 3.3.16 and below, as well as 4.0.0-a2, and is exploitable in the default configuration with a single unauthenticated HTTP request to obtain a valid session as any user. phpBB released version 3.3.17 after the issue was reported through HackerOne on June 2, with a working patch published on June 6. The update also introduces a breaking change for OAuth authentication users because the redirect URI handler has moved to /user/oauth/authenticate/..., which may affect deployments relying on existing OAuth flows. Ivanti also disclosed two critical Sentry secure mobile gateway vulnerabilities. CVE-2026-10520 is a maximum-severity OS command injection flaw allowing remote code execution as root, while CVE-2026-10523 is an authentication bypass that can allow unauthenticated remote attackers to create rogue administrative accounts and gain full administrative access; Ivanti stated it had no evidence of exploitation at disclosure time. ServiceNow reported that unknown threat actors exploited a security issue to gain greater unauthorized access to susceptible customer instances than intended. The issue does not currently have a CVE identifier, and details first emerged publicly through Reddit before ServiceNow described anomalous activity and successful queries of instance tables affecting a subset of customers. ServiceNow applied a hosted-instance security update on June 5, changing an endpoint configuration to restrict access to authenticated users. The affected population includes customers on the Australia platform release and customers that made certain configuration changes to instances running releases prior to Australia. The incident is notable because it involves exploitation against a major enterprise workflow platform and successful access to customer instance data. ServiceNow said affected customers were notified after evidence of successful table queries was observed. Anthropic released Claude Fable 5 publicly while limiting its cyber-unrestricted counterpart, Claude Mythos 5, to vetted cyber defenders and critical infrastructure operators. The company said Mythos-class models can find and exploit software vulnerabilities at a level where unrestricted public access would provide attackers with significant uplift. Claude Fable 5 uses safety classifiers that route flagged cyber, biology, chemistry, and distillation requests to the weaker Claude Opus 4.8 rather than refusing them outright. Anthropic also said that during Project Glasswing, it and roughly 50 partners used Claude Mythos Preview to identify more than ten thousand high- or critical-severity vulnerabilities in systemically important software. Anthropic will require 30-day data retention for traffic on Fable 5, Mythos 5, and future models at this capability level, while stating that retained data will not be used for training or non-safety purposes. The release reflects a widening gap between public AI capabilities and restricted cyber-specialized systems intended for controlled defensive use.

item.title: Proto6 protobuf.js RCE Flaws and AI Security Tooling Developments
item.description: This digest window is dominated by two themes: newly disclosed Node.js ecosystem risk in protobuf.js, and continued maturation of AI tooling for security operations and research. The most immediately operationally relevant item is the Proto6 vulnerability set, which affects protobuf.js consumers...
item.content:encoded: This digest window is dominated by two themes: newly disclosed Node.js ecosystem risk in protobuf.js, and continued maturation of AI tooling for security operations and research. The most immediately operationally relevant item is the Proto6 vulnerability set, which affects protobuf.js consumers across application, cloud library, messaging, and CI/CD contexts. Researchers at Cyera disclosed six vulnerabilities collectively named Proto6 in protobuf.js, a widely used JavaScript implementation for Protocol Buffers. The issues can expose affected Node.js applications to remote code execution and denial-of-service conditions, particularly where applications deserialize Protobuf data or generate code from schemas. The flaws center on protobuf.js treating schema and metadata as trusted by default. According to the reporting, this behavior can allow attacker-controlled schema-related input to influence application behavior and, in certain paths, reach code execution conditions. The most severe issue highlighted is CVE-2026-44291, which can allow arbitrary JavaScript execution when attacker-controlled input reaches a prototype pollution gadget before protobuf.js encodes or decodes a message. The affected exposure surface extends beyond direct protobuf.js use. The article identifies Google Cloud client libraries, messaging frameworks such as Baileys, Node.js services handling Protobuf data, and CI/CD pipelines that generate code from schemas as potentially impacted environments. Fixes are reported in protobufjs 7.5.6 and 8.0.2, along with protobufjs-cli 1.2.1 and 2.0.2. Several articles in this window focused on AI-enabled cybersecurity tooling and model governance rather than active intrusion activity. SpecterOps introduced GhostWorks, an AI-focused engineering and research initiative intended to test frontier AI capabilities against identity-security problems, document limitations, and transition successful prototypes beyond the lab. The initiative follows earlier SpecterOps AI work, including OpenAI adversarial red teaming and participation in OpenAI’s Trusted Access for Cyber program. SpecterOps also published a technical examination of GEPA, an optimization framework for refining prompts using LLM inference instead of reinforcement learning. The proof-of-concept optimized an AGENTS.md prompt for a web CTF agent with shell execution, file access, web request, and flag submission tooling. Its evaluation function rewarded solved challenges and weighted turn efficiency more heavily than runtime, using agent run artifacts such as reasoning output, tool use, return values, and errors to identify patterns. The GEPA demonstration produced mixed but informative results: one challenge score decreased slightly while two improved, with signs of overfitting attributed to the small training and validation set. The article positions GEPA as measurable prompt engineering for security agents, especially where practitioners use closed-source or remote-hosted models and have limited compute resources. Separately, Anthropic began rolling out Claude Fable 5, described as a safer version of a Mythos-class model with guardrails for sensitive domains including offensive cybersecurity, biology, and chemistry. The unrestricted Claude Mythos 5 model is reportedly limited to highly vetted trusted partners, including government cyberdefenders and selected life sciences researchers. BleepingComputer reported high usage consumption during testing, including approximately 1 million tokens in 8 minutes and rapid exhaustion of a $100 Max subscription daily allowance when using Workflow mode and high model thinking.

item.title: Ivanti Sentry CVE-2026-10520 RCE and Microsoft Defender RoguePlanet Zero-Day
item.description: This digest centers on two high-impact vulnerability disclosures affecting widely deployed enterprise security technologies: Ivanti Sentry and Microsoft Defender. The reporting highlights a pre-authentication root-level remote code execution path in Ivanti Sentry and a publicly released Microsoft...
item.content:encoded: This digest centers on two high-impact vulnerability disclosures affecting widely deployed enterprise security technologies: Ivanti Sentry and Microsoft Defender. The reporting highlights a pre-authentication root-level remote code execution path in Ivanti Sentry and a publicly released Microsoft Defender local privilege escalation exploit capable of spawning SYSTEM shells on fully patched Windows systems. WatchTowr Labs published technical analysis of CVE-2026-10520, an OS command injection vulnerability in Ivanti Sentry versions before R10.5.2, R10.6.2, and R10.7.1. According to the analysis, the vulnerable unauthenticated endpoint is /mics/api/v2/sentry/mics-config/handleMessage, where a user-controlled message parameter is passed into backend configuration handling logic. The issue can allow a remote unauthenticated attacker to achieve root-level remote code execution. The researchers described how the message value is parsed as a MICS configuration command into fields including command, module, XPath, and XML body. They found that the EXECUTE path could reach executeNativeCommand through reflection-based backend handling, and reproduced the vulnerability by sending a POST request containing a commandexec XML block with uname -a in the reqandres field. The HTTP response returned command output, demonstrating practical exploitation of the flaw. The patched Ivanti implementation reportedly hardcodes the command passed to handleMessage and adds Apache configuration rules that block access to the vulnerable endpoint, redirecting unauthenticated requests to the login page. The analysis is notable because it provides endpoint-level and execution-path detail for a vulnerability that sits on an exposed management/security product boundary, where unauthenticated reachability materially increases operational risk. BleepingComputer reported on RoguePlanet, a Microsoft Defender zero-day exploit released by researcher Nightmare Eclipse. The exploit is described as a race condition that can spawn a command prompt with SYSTEM privileges on fully patched Windows 10 and Windows 11 devices, though its reliability varies by system. ThreatLocker told BleepingComputer it reproduced the exploit on fully patched Windows 11 systems with KB5094126 installed and confirmed it worked as described. Nightmare Eclipse said RoguePlanet was originally developed as a remote code execution vulnerability involving Microsoft Defender’s handling of files on remote SMB shares. The researcher claimed Microsoft silently hardened Defender in mid-May to block junction attacks, changing the exploit’s practical impact from the originally described RCE scenario to a local privilege escalation path. The release also forms part of an ongoing public dispute between Nightmare Eclipse and Microsoft over vulnerability disclosure and bug bounty practices, following previous releases named BlueHammer, RedSun, GreenPlasma, and YellowKey. Together, these disclosures underscore continued scrutiny of defensive and mobile infrastructure products as high-value exploit targets. One case involves unauthenticated command execution in an enterprise gateway product, while the other concerns privilege escalation in a default Windows security component, creating distinct but operationally significant exposure patterns for defenders tracking exploit development and public proof-of-concept activity.

item.title: Microsoft Patch Tuesday, Veeam CVE-2026-44963, and Miasma Supply-Chain Fallout
item.description: During this coverage window, reporting was dominated by large-scale vulnerability remediation, software supply-chain compromise, and the accelerating role of AI in vulnerability discovery. Microsoft addressed 200 flaws including three publicly disclosed zero-days, while Veeam patched a critical...
item.content:encoded: During this coverage window, reporting was dominated by large-scale vulnerability remediation, software supply-chain compromise, and the accelerating role of AI in vulnerability discovery. Microsoft addressed 200 flaws including three publicly disclosed zero-days, while Veeam patched a critical Backup & Replication RCE issue affecting domain-joined servers. Separate reporting detailed fallout from the Miasma/Shai-Hulud ecosystem and new malware and authentication research relevant to defenders. Microsoft's June 2026 Patch Tuesday fixed 200 vulnerabilities, including 33 critical issues and three publicly disclosed zero-days: CVE-2026-45586 in Windows Collaborative Translation Framework, CVE-2026-49160 in HTTP.sys, and CVE-2026-50507 in Windows BitLocker. Microsoft said none of the three zero-days were known to have been exploited. The release also introduced a MaxHeadersCount registry setting tied to mitigation of the HTTP/2 Bomb denial-of-service issue affecting HTTP.sys. Microsoft also released mandatory Windows 11 cumulative updates KB5094126 and KB5093998 for supported 25H2/24H2 and 23H2 systems. The updates include security fixes, reliability improvements, and feature changes such as Shared Audio enhancements, Task Manager NPU visibility, Multi-App Camera policy controls, and setup-time custom user folder naming. Microsoft reported no new known issues for this Patch Tuesday release. Veeam released fixes for CVE-2026-44963, a critical Backup & Replication vulnerability that can allow remote code execution on domain-joined backup servers. The issue affects version 12.3.2.4465 and earlier version 12 builds and is fixed in 12.3.2.4854, while version 13.x is not affected due to architectural changes. Veeam said exploitation requires any low-privileged authenticated domain user and noted there were no reports of active exploitation. Microsoft confirmed that some GitHub repositories were temporarily removed after a security incident involving 73 open-source projects compromised to inject an information stealer. Some repositories were restored after review, while others remained offline as the investigation continued. Microsoft said a small number of customers who may have pulled affected content were notified through support channels. Earlier reporting said the removed repositories spanned Azure, microsoft, Azure-Samples, and MicrosoftDocs organizations and briefly disrupted CI workflows, including Azure/functions-action usage. Researchers linked the incident to broader Miasma/Shai-Hulud supply-chain activity involving Microsoft GitHub resources, Red Hat npm packages, PyPI packages, and durabletask. Analysis of the Miasma payload found it could trigger automatic code execution when an affected repository was opened in an AI-powered coding tool or IDE. StepSecurity introduced a Threat Center API endpoint that returns compromised open source components associated with a specific incident. The endpoint provides ecosystem, component, affected version, severity, verification status, description, and threat intelligence identifiers. The company described use cases including SIEM enrichment, SOC workflow automation, and correlation against SBOMs or lockfiles. XBOW reported testing Anthropic's Mythos Preview across offensive security benchmarks, workflows, integrations, and API-based agents. The testing found the model stronger than prior systems at identifying vulnerability candidates, particularly with source code access, while live-site interaction remained important for exploitability validation. XBOW also reported strong results in native-code vulnerability discovery, reverse engineering, and browser-based visual workflows, with mixed judgment and high estimated cost. Mitiga highlighted Anthropic reporting that Project Glasswing partners used Mythos Preview to find more than 10,000 high- or critical-severity vulnerabilities in systemically important software. The article argued that the bottleneck has shifted from discovery to verification, disclosure, and patching, citing 75 patched issues out of 530 disclosed high- or critical-severity bugs at the time of writing. It also framed modern exposure around pre-patch exploitation timelines and very short eCrime breakout times. Health-ISAC discussed President Trump's AI executive order through a healthcare cybersecurity lens. Errol Weiss said the sector's opportunity lies in faster coordination, especially as attackers use AI to compress the time from vulnerability discovery to exploitation. The article emphasized timely, actionable information sharing through ISAC channels and measurable resilience improvements for critical infrastructure. Zscaler ThreatLabz analyzed MLTBackdoor, a new malware family identified in May 2026 and assessed as likely connected to a ransomware-related actor. The malware was delivered through a multi-stage ClickFix chain using endpointdlp.dll and an RC4-encrypted data.bin payload. It uses heavy obfuscation, API hashing, indirect system calls, anti-analysis checks, filesystem commands, a Beacon Object File loader, and encrypted TLS-based command-and-control over port 443. SpecterOps published research on Kerberos user-to-user authentication in Windows environments. The article explains how U2U encrypts a service ticket with a target user's TGT session key, including the KERB-TGT-REQUEST and ENC-TKT-IN-SKEY flow. It also details RDP Network Level Authentication usage during CredSSP negotiation and how UnPAC-the-Hash combines PKINIT and U2U to recover a user's NT hash from PACCREDENTIALINFO.
